title: New leaders in Peru and Colombia may find it hard to deliver on their promises
gist: Peru and Colombia elected right-wing populist governments in June 2026, but razor-thin mandates and record political polarization mean neither Keiko Fujimori nor Abelardo de la Espriella is likely to govern effectively or deliver on promises. Historical evidence shows populist strong-hand rule tends to erode institutions and lower income per capita over time.

insights.claim: Peru and Colombia each elected new right-wing presidents in June 2026 by extremely narrow margins, with Colombia's Abelardo de la Espriella winning 49.7% to 48.7% and Keiko Fujimori's Peru count stretching on for days.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Wafer-thin mandates signal weak governing capacity and elevated political risk for investors weighing Andean exposure over the coming administrations.
insights.quote: De la Espriella won by 49.7 per cent to 48.7.
insights.locator: In the space of two weeks in June

insights.claim: Political polarization in Latin America has grown faster than in any other region and now exceeds the global average, per the UNDP's 2026 regional report.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Structural polarization raises the base rate of governance deadlock and policy reversal risk across the region, not just in these two countries.

insights.claim: Polarization creates public demand for strong-hand leaders while simultaneously destroying the conditions those leaders need to succeed: broad coalitions, patient reform, and credible institutions.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: This self-defeating dynamic is the core thesis explaining why populist wins repeatedly fail to translate into delivery, a pattern investors can generalize.
insights.quote: Polarization manufactures the demand for strong hands while destroying the supply of what the evidence says works: broad coalitions, patient reform, credible institutions.

themes.theme: Peru: macro anchors versus political drag

insights.claim: Peru retains credible macroeconomic anchors including an independent central bank, one of the region's lowest public debt ratios, inflation converging to target, and a mining project pipeline worth roughly $60 billion.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Strong fundamentals and a $60bn mining pipeline make Peru attractive on paper, but political instability is the binding constraint to watch, not the macro.
insights.quote: a pipeline of new mining projects worth some $60 billion

insights.claim: Peru's potential growth has fallen from 5.5% annually during the 2004-2013 commodity boom to closer to 2.5%, dragged down by a public administration weakened by constant turnover.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The halving of trend growth quantifies the cost of political dysfunction and caps upside for Peru-linked assets absent institutional repair.
insights.locator: But politics has been highly unstable

insights.claim: A very strong El Niño weather system is underway in Peru in 2026 with a 63% probability of reaching 'very strong' magnitude, and past events have cost between 0.7% and 5.3% of GDP.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A material near-term GDP shock (up to 5.3%) that could hit agriculture, fishing and infrastructure just as a weak government takes office.
insights.locator: In addition, forecasters confirm an El Niño

insights.claim: Fujimori inherits an insolvent state oil company and multibillion-dollar arbitration claims against the Peruvian state.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Contingent fiscal liabilities and a broken state energy asset are hidden balance-sheet risks for sovereign credit watchers.
insights.locator: Fujimori also inherits an insolvent state oil company

insights.claim: Colombia faces sluggish growth, fiscal deficits and public debt near historical highs, with the IMF advising a fiscal consolidation of roughly three points of GDP.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A required 3-point-of-GDP consolidation is politically hard for a divided congress, keeping Colombian fiscal and rating risk elevated.
insights.locator: Colombia, a larger and more diversified economy

insights.claim: Colombia's security is deteriorating: a leading presidential candidate was assassinated during the campaign, illegal armed groups have grown to around 27,000 members, and crime has displaced the economy as citizens' top concern.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Worsening security and stalled peace implementation raise country risk premia and threaten investment across extractives and infrastructure.
insights.quote: Illegal armed groups have grown to around 27,000 members

insights.claim: Neither new president commands a legislative majority, making congress rather than the runoff the real test of governability in both countries.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Coalition math, not the presidential result, determines whether reforms pass; watch congressional alignment as the leading governance indicator.
insights.quote: Both presidents will quickly discover that runoff elections are easier to win than decisive votes in their legislatures.
insights.locator: Congress is the real second round

insights.claim: Peru's new bicameral Congress imposes Senate supermajority requirements that, for the first time in decades, act as a brake on the impeachment habit that removed or forced out every president since 2016.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Structural reform reducing impeachment churn could modestly improve Peruvian policy continuity and political-risk pricing if it holds.
insights.locator: Peru's new bicameral Congress seats six parties

insights.claim: Latinobarometro data shows democratic satisfaction is very low, with just 10% of Peruvians satisfied with how their democracy works and satisfaction barely reaching 20% in Colombia, while 'expectations pressure' is at its widest since 1995.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Record expectations pressure means voters will not tolerate underperformance, raising odds of social unrest and mid-term political reversals.
insights.quote: electorates will not tolerate underperformance by the new governments

insights.claim: The most comprehensive study available, covering 120 years of populist governments worldwide, finds income per capita roughly 10% lower after fifteen years of populist rule, driven by eroded institutions and collapsed investment.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A hard, quantified base rate: populist governance destroys ~10% of income over 15 years, a structural headwind for long-horizon country allocation.
insights.quote: finds income per capita roughly ten per cent lower after fifteen years of populist rule, through eroded institutions and collapsed investment

insights.claim: El Salvador's homicide collapse and high democratic satisfaction are unlikely to be replicable in the Andes, where transnational criminal economies built on illegal gold and cocaine command export markets that would survive even mass arrests.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Undercuts the 'Bukele model' as a template for the Andes; investors should discount promises of rapid security-led turnarounds there.
insights.locator: In El Salvador homicides have collapsed
